perturbation.original_text: 5. The receiving party's duties under Section 2 of this Agreement expire with respect to any particular item of INFORMATION five years after the date of disclosure hereunder to the receiving party, or three years after the expiration or termination of the relationship between the parties to this Agreement, whichever is later.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. The receiving party's duties under Section 2 of this Agreement expire with respect to any particular item of INFORMATION one year after the date of disclosure hereunder to the receiving party, or six months after the expiration or termination of the relationship between the parties to this Agreement, whichever is later.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year after disclosure and six months after the relationship ends could be deemed unreasonable, potentially conflicting with California's trade secret protection laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: California law protects trade secrets, which can include confidential information. A one-year protection period might be considered insufficient to protect trade secrets, especially given the nature of the information described in the agreement. This violates the principle that trade secrets must be protected for a period reasonably necessary to eliminate any commercial advantage the misappropriator obtained.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period for trade secrets to one year after disclosure and six months after the relationship ends. The Law Explanation states that this shorter period "might be considered insufficient to protect trade secrets." This implies that the modification creates a legal contradiction because it potentially violates California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act, as it may not provide a period reasonably necessary to eliminate any commercial advantage obtained by a misappropriator.

perturbation.original_text: 4. The receiving party shall, upon request of the disclosing party: (i) return to the disclosing party all documents, drawings, equipment and other tangible materials, including all INFORMATION and all manifestations thereof, delivered to the receiving party under this Agreement, and all copies and reproductions thereof; and (ii) certify to the disclosing party that all such INFORMATION has been returned.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. The receiving party shall, at their discretion, return to the disclosing party documents, drawings, equipment and other tangible materials, including all INFORMATION and all manifestations thereof, delivered to the receiving party under this Agreement, and all copies and reproductions thereof; and (ii) certification of return is optional.
perturbation.explanation: Making the return of confidential information discretionary and optional for certification could conflict with the duty of care required under California law for protecting trade secrets. This violates legal standards requiring reasonable efforts to safeguard confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3426.1 allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. Making the return of materials discretionary weakens the disclosing party's ability to prevent misappropriation and seek injunctive relief, potentially conflicting with the intent of the statute to protect trade secrets actively.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text makes the return of confidential information discretionary and certification optional, which directly conflicts with the need to protect trade secrets as outlined in California Civil Code § 3426.1. The original contract mandates return and certification, while the modification weakens this protection. The scraped snippet from California Code, CIV 3426.1, defines 'Misappropriation' which involves the unauthorized use of trade secrets, emphasizing the legal importance of safeguarding such information. By making the return of materials discretionary, the modified contract undermines the disclosing party's ability to prevent misappropriation and seek injunctive relief, conflicting with the statute's intent to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: (g) This Agreement is governed by and will be construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California, and the state and federal courts of California shall be the exclusive forum.
perturbation.changed_text: (g) This Agreement is governed by and will be construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, and disputes arising under this agreement will be subject to mandatory arbitration in Italy.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware and requiring mandatory arbitration in Italy may violate California's public policy if the agreement significantly impacts California residents or businesses, especially regarding trade secret protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1296 addresses recognition and enforcement of foreign arbitration agreements. California courts may refuse to enforce an arbitration agreement if it violates California's public policy. If the agreement involves the protection of California trade secrets or significantly impacts California interests, mandating arbitration in Italy under Delaware law could be deemed against public policy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1296. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 3. OF SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS OF A CIVIL NATURE [1063 - 1822.60] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9.2. PUBLIC CONSTRUCTION CONTRACT ARBITRATION [1296- 1296.] ( Title 9.2 added by Stats. 1979, Ch. 46. ) 1296. The parties to a construction contract with a public agency may expressly agree in writing that in any arbitration to resolve a dispute relating to the contract, the arbitrator’s award shall be supported by law and substantial evidence. If the agreement so provides, a court shall, subject to Section 1286.4, vacate the award if after review of the award it determines either that the award is not supported by substantial evidence or that it is based on an error of law. (Added by Stats. 1979, Ch. 46.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a potential legal contradiction. By changing the governing law to Delaware and mandating arbitration in Italy, the agreement may undermine California's public policy, particularly if it involves California trade secrets or significantly affects California interests. This is based on the law explanation, which directly cites California Code of Civil Procedure § 1296, suggesting California courts might refuse to enforce arbitration agreements violating its public policy. The scraped snippet, CCP 1296, discusses arbitration related to public agencies. While the snippet itself doesn't directly address international arbitration or choice of law, the LLM explanation suggests that the change from California law and courts to Delaware law and mandatory arbitration in Italy could be seen as against California's public policy, especially if it negatively impacts California's interests.

perturbation.original_text: 2. The receiving party shall hold in trust and confidence, and not disclose to others, by any means, any and all INFORMATION disclosed under this Agreement. INFORMATION may be used by the receiving party only for the purpose of considering or pursuing a business relationship or business transaction with the disclosing party. The receiving party may disclose INFORMATION received under this Agreement to persons within its organization who have a need to know such information and only if such persons are bound in writing (pursuant, for example to a general employee non-disclosure agreement protecting third party confidential information as well as the employer's confidential information) to protect the confidentiality of such INFORMATION. The receiving party further agrees it shall take the same measures, but no less than reasonable security measures, and use the same care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, to preserve and protect the secrecy of, and to avoid disclosure or unauthorized use of, the disclosing party's INFORMATION as it uses with its own information of similar importance. With respect to tangible materials constituting INFORMATION the receiving party agrees not to analyze any such materials for composition or structure.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. The receiving party shall endeavor to hold information in confidence, but is not liable for accidental disclosure. The receiving party may use INFORMATION for any purpose. The receiving party may disclose INFORMATION received under this Agreement to persons within its organization or any third party with no restrictions. The receiving party agrees to take measures they deem appropriate to protect the INFORMATION. With respect to tangible materials constituting INFORMATION the receiving party is encouraged, but not required to refrain from analyzing any such materials for composition or structure.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the obligation to hold information in trust, allowing use for any purpose, removing restrictions on disclosure, and weakening security measures significantly weakens trade secret protection, potentially violating California law requiring reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3426.1 allows for injunctive relief for misappropriation of trade secrets. By removing the obligation to hold information in confidence and allowing unrestricted disclosure, this modification directly conflicts with the necessary steps to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets, undermining the basis for injunctive relief.

As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens trade secret protection, creating a legal contradiction. By removing the obligation to hold information in trust, allowing use for any purpose, removing restrictions on disclosure, and weakening security measures, the changed text undermines the 'duty to maintain secrecy'. California Civil Code § 3426.1 defines 'improper means' to include 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy.' The modifications in the changed text facilitate such a breach, directly conflicting with the requirement to protect trade secrets, thus creating a legal contradiction.
